coredata.dc:title: A reagentless electrochemical biosensor based on thionine wrapped E. coli and chitosan-entrapped carbon nanodots film modified glassy carbon electrode for wastewater toxicity assessment
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Deyu
Gao, Guanyue
Shen, Jie
Yu, Yuan
Zhi, Jinfang
coredata.dc:description: An effective way to prepare the reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor has been developed. First, the mediator thionine (TH) is wrapped on the surface of E. coli (E. coli@TH) through the simple electrostatic interaction, which makes E. coli and TH closely together and therefore shortens the electron transfer distance between the electroactive centers and the electrode. Then, E. coli@TH together with chitosan (CHI)-entrapped carbon nanodots (CNDs)are immobilized onto the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surfaces to form the reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor (CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH), in which the CNDs are used to improve the conductivity of the modified electrode, and further enhance the electron transfer from E. coli to the electrode. The reagentless CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH biosensor is finally used to evaluate the toxicity of four heavy metal ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) and three real wastewater samples, showing good performance in the evaluation of wastewater toxicity compared to other reported methods. This work opens up a new way for the preparation of reagentless electrodes, making it possible for electrochemical microbial biosensors to be equipped and realize on-line detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thionine
E. coli
Reagentless biosensor
Toxicity
Wastewater

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: E. coli cultivation and preparation of thionine “wrapped” E. coli (E. coli@TH)
Fabrication of reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensors
Characterizations of TH ‘wrapped’ E. coli (E. coli@TH)
Characterization of CNDs and CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE
Electrochemical characteristics of different electrochemical biosensors
Analytical Performance of the Biosensor for acute total toxicity assessment
Application for toxicity assessment of heavy metal ions
Application of the biosensor for toxicity assessment of three wastewater samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) images of E. coli and E. coli@TH. (a) and (b) are the images of E. coli and E. coli@TH when white light was used as incident light, respectively. (c) and (d) are the images of E. coli and E. coli@TH when 561nm laser was used as incident light, respectively.
(a) TEM images of the prepared CNDs; (b) Low and (d) high magnification SEM images of the CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE.

(a and b) The CV curves for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE (line a1 and a2), CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE (line b1 and b2) and CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE (line c1 and c2) in PBS (pH=7.0, 0.1M) in absence (Fig. 3a) and presence (Fig. 3b) of respiration substrate (RS). Scan rate was 50mV/s. (c) The CV curves for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE in RS solution at different scan rates (from inner to outer: 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300mV/s). Inset plot of the anodic (Ipa) and cathodic (Ipc) peak currents vs. scan rate. (d) EIS spectra obtained at bare GCE, CHI-E. coli@TH/GCE and CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE in 5mMK4[Fe(CN)6]/K3[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1M KCl.

Electrochemical responses of CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE, CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE and CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE to Cd2+ ion (15mg/L). The chronoamperometry were performed at 0.3V vs Ag/AgCl under continuous and constant magnetic stirring in 10mL RS solution. Cd2+ ion were added at 500s.
The relationships between the inhibitory percentages and concentrations of heavy metal ions. Noting log transformation applied on concentration (x) axis.
Scheme representation for the preparation of E. coli@TH and fabrication of integrated chitosan (CHI)-carbon nanodots (CNDs)-E. coli@TH modified glassy carbon electrode (CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE).
Comparisons of the IC50 values of Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ achieved by our approach with those obtained by other methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Each entry in Table represents the the mean (±S.D.) of three determinations.

head.ce:title: A reagentless electrochemical biosensor based on thionine wrapped E. coli and chitosan-entrapped carbon nanodots film modified glassy carbon electrode for wastewater toxicity assessment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No.29 Zhongguancundonglu, Haidian District, Beijing, 100190, PR China Tel.: +86 01082543537.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An effective way to prepare the reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor has been developed. First, the mediator thionine (TH) is wrapped on the surface of E. coli (E. coli@TH) through the simple electrostatic interaction, which makes E. coli and TH closely together and therefore shortens the electron transfer distance between the electroactive centers and the electrode. Then, E. coli@TH together with chitosan (CHI)-entrapped carbon nanodots (CNDs)are immobilized onto the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surfaces to form the reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor (CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH), in which the CNDs are used to improve the conductivity of the modified electrode, and further enhance the electron transfer from E. coli to the electrode. The reagentless CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH biosensor is finally used to evaluate the toxicity of four heavy metal ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) and three real wastewater samples, showing good performance in the evaluation of wastewater toxicity compared to other reported methods. This work opens up a new way for the preparation of reagentless electrodes, making it possible for electrochemical microbial biosensors to be equipped and realize on-line detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution is of a wide concern and may be subjected to prescriptive legislation. Enforcement of environmental standards demands accurate and cost effective monitoring techniques. Conventional techniques offer high precision but are disadvantaged by the high cost of specialized equipment, the need for trained personnel, and are not amenable to rapid on-site analysis [1,2]. Hence, there is a general need to develop simple, rapid and yet inexpensive environmental monitoring tests for use by regulatory agencies. Much work has been carried out in developing new analytical tools that incorporate the specificity of biological assays to reflect organisms/ecosystem health [3–5]. As a holistic approach, the bioassays can provide a toxicity evaluation of the total integrated effect of all constituent components, including toxicants and confounding variables in a given complex sample matrix. The past decades have witnessed the trend to develop effective means to assess the total toxicity of chemicals in wastewater [6,7]. Considerable research has been done to evaluate the toxicity of the chemicals by microbes. Several microbes-based bioassays for water toxicity analysis have been described and these include bioassays based on the measurement of microbial enzyme activity [6], oxygen consumption [8,9], bioluminescence intensity [7], nitrification activity [10,11], etc. Among these toxicity evaluation methods, transforming the microbial respiration intensity to electrochemical signal for toxicity assessment has drawn much attention due to its convenience for instrumentation and fast detection period [2,12].

Because most microbes are unable to exchange electrons to electrode directly, artificial redox mediators are widely used for the conversion of microbial respiration intensity to electrochemical signals [13]. The so-called redox mediators are some electrochemical active substances which can participate in microbial respiration process and accept the electrons from electron transport chain (ETC) like oxygen [14,15]. Thus, the currents of reduced mediators by microbes can reflect the microbial activity and the inhibition by toxicants can be detected as the resulting change of currents. Some mediators, such as neutral red [16], ferricyanide [14], p-benzoquinone [17], etc., have been used in microbial reactions. However, as mediator dissolves in solution, the leaching susceptibility of the soluble mediator to the sample solution, diffusion barrier of the mediator between electrode interface, and that exposure of reference electrode to mediator solution can contaminate the surface of the electrode and alter the detection potential, are some drawbacks affecting stability and reproducibility. More importantly, electron transfer process is dependent on the distance between the redox center and the electrode, and making their distance as close as possible is an important factor to realize fast and even direct electron transfer [18]. Immobilization of mediators can shorten the distance between the active center and electrode, which increases the electron tunneling rate efficiently. Accordingly, immobilization of mediators on electrode surface and further construction of reagentless biosensors have become a tendency [19–21]. Usually, the conventional method for construction of reagentless biosensors is the combination of mediator and biorecognition unit (i.e., microbe) into one integrated unit. The possibility to immobilize all components (microbe, mediator) in the polymer layer in an active and durable form gives rise to reagentless biosensor with extended operational stability [22–24].

Thionine (TH), an organic dye mediator, has attracted much attention in electrochemical biosensors by virtue of their high electron transfer efficiency and its formal potential is between −0.25V and 0.08V, near the redox potential of many biomolecules [25,26]. E. coli, the most common Gram-negative bacterium, has been widely used for toxicity assessment of chemicals [27,28]. In present work, we report a new reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor based on ‘wrapping’ E. coli with thionine (E. coli@TH) as the biorecognition unit through the electrostatic interaction, and then E. coli@TH is immobilized into chitosan (CHI)-entrapped carbon nanodots (CNDs) film and further modified on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) to form the reagentless CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH electrochemical microbial biosensor. To our best knowledge, it is the first time that mediator is ‘wrapped’ on the surface of microbe for electron wiring. The utilization of CNDs enhances the conductivity of the modified electrode and further improves the electron transfer between E. coli and the electrode. The biosensor is finally used to assess the toxicity of four heavy metal ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) and three wastewater samples. The results have been compared with other electrochemical methods based on respiration inhibition of microbes and ISO methods for toxicity assessment.

Thionine acetate was obtained from Alfa Aesa. Peptone and beef extract were from Beijing Aoboxing Bio-tech Co., Ltd., China. All other chemicals were from Beijing Lanyi Chemical Products Co., Ltd., China.0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with pH=7.0 was prepared by mixing 0.1M Na2HPO4·12H2O and 0.1M NaH2PO4·2H2O together. The pH value was adjusted to 7.0 by adding 1M HCl or 1M NaOH. A 0.2% (w/V) chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving chitosan flakes in 1% (V/V) acetic acid solution. The culture medium (10g/L peptone, 3g/L beef extract, 5g/L NaCl) for E. coli was adjusted to pH=7.3–7.5with NaOH (2mol/L) and sterilized by autoclaving. The respiration substrate (RS) solution was prepared by mixing glucose, sodium succinate and sodium lactate with each of 10mM in PBS (pH=7.0, 0.1M).All chemicals were of analytical grade and all solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water.

The concentration of E. coli suspension (OD600) was measured by using a UV spectrophotometer (SECOMAM UVIKONXL) at 600nm. TEM observation was performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Scanning electron microscopes (SEM) of the prepared electrodes were carried out on a QUANTAN FEG 250 operated at 10kV. The specimens were coated with a layer of gold by ion sputtering before SEM observations. The zeta potentials were measured by photon correlation spectroscopy using a Zetasizer Nano instrument (Malvern Instruments, ZEN3600). Confocal laser scanning microscopes (CLSM) was observed on Nikon-C2-SIM confocal microscopy. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with a FRD 100 frequency response detector (Princeton, USA).

CNDs were prepared with candle soot as starting material according to the reported methods [29,30]. Candle soot was collected first by sitting a glass plate on the top of a lighted candle. The height between the glass plate and tip of the flame was about 0.5cm. Then, 30mg candle soot was suspended in 20mL of water/ethanol mixture (1:1) with the help of ultrasonication. The black mixture was centrifuged twice at 3500rpm (1230× g) for 4min to separate out large-size carbon soot particles. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged again at 6000rpm (3620× g) for 10min. The black precipitate was collected and dried at 37°C. For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation, CNDs were first dispersed in ethanol by ultrasonication for 10min. and then added onto carbon-coated copper grids and dried at 60°C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: E. coli cultivation and preparation of thionine “wrapped” E. coli (E. coli@TH)
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli (ATCC 25922) was obtained from China General Microbiological Culture Collection Center (CGMCC) and replanted regularly on nutrient agar plates to ensure its viability. The cell biomass was prepared by aerobic cultivation at 37°C on a rotary shaker in 250mL flasks filled with 100mL of medium for 16h. The growth medium contained: 10gL−1 peptone, 5gL−1 beef extract, and 5gL−1 NaCl. The cells were harvested by centrifuging at 6000rpm for 5min at room temperature, and then washed twice with 0.85% (w/v) saline solution and finally resuspended to OD600 =3.0. To prepare E. coli@TH, 5mL E. coli suspension and 20mL TH solution (0.3mM) were mixed and kept for 10min. Then, the suspension was centrifuged three times at 6000rpm for 5min and resuspended to 1mL to obtain the E. coli@TH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of E. coli@TH preparation and fabrication of chitosan (CHI)-carbon nanodots (CNDs)-E. coli@TH modified glassy carbon electrode (defined as CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE) is presented in Scheme 1 . Before modification, bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 1, 0.3, 0.05μm alumina powders in sequence, then ultrasonicated successively with 1:1 nitric acid, acetone and deionized water and finally dried at room temperature. To fabricate the CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE, 40μL of chitosan solution, 40μL of E. coli@TH solution and certain volumes of CNDs solution (0.2mg/mL) were mixed thoroughly first, and then an aliquot of 10μL of the mixed solution was deposited onto GCE, and finally dried at ambient condition. The modified GCE was stored at 4°C before use. Meanwhile, CHI-E. coli/GCE, CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE and CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE were also fabricated for comparisons according to the procedure mentioned above. But it should be noted that to obtain the CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE, TH was mixed directly with CHI, CNDs and E. coli and then deposited on the electrode.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry were performed with a 263A Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Princeton, USA) with a three electrode system composed of a Pt wire as an auxiliary electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode, and a bare GCE electrode or a modified GCE electrode as the working electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with a FRD 100 frequency response detector (Princeton, USA) connected to the same potentiostat/galvanostat. The surface area of the working electrode was 0.08cm2. All measurements were carried out at room temperature. The toxicity assessments were performed by chronoamperometry (0.3V vs Ag/AgCl) under continuous and constant magnetic stirring in the respiration substrate (RS) solution. After about 500s for stabilization, heavy metal ions or wastewater samples were added into the solution. The inhibition ratio is adopted to evaluate the toxicity of added toxicants, which is calculated by Eq. (1).
(1) Inhibition (%)=(1−iexp/icon)×100%
where icon is the steady current before the addition of heavy metal ions, iexp is the steady current after the addition of heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of TH ‘wrapped’ E. coli (E. coli@TH)
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that TH is not only a good redox mediator, but also an organic fluorescence dye due to the π-conjugated azine rings in its structure [31,32]. Fig. S1 shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of TH. The maximum excitation wavelength is about 561nm (line a) and the maximum emission wavelength is about 643nm (line b), suggesting TH emits red fluorescence. Taking advantage of the TH fluorescence property, the state E. coli@TH can be fully investigated by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, both bare E. coli and E. coli@TH keep their typical rod-shape morphology. Besides, comparing to the bare E. coli (Fig. 1c), E.coli@TH show well-distributed red fluorescence layer around the E. coli (Fig. 1d), indicating E. coli cells are indeed wrapped by TH uniformly, and the TH wrapping layer does not damage the shape of E. coli. The zeta potentials (ζ) of E. coli before and after TH wrapping are demonstrated in Fig. S2. A positively shift of ζ can be observed from −42.3mV of bare E. coli to −21.9mV of E. coli@TH, i.e., cationic dye TH adsorb on to the negative charges E. coli surfaces, suggesting the adsorption effect of TH molecules on the surface of E. coli is resulting from the electrostatic interaction. All these results suggest TH molecules are successful ‘wrapped’ on the surface of E. coli cells through their electrostatic interaction as expected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CNDs and CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Immobilization of microbes and mediators without leaching from electrode surface is crucial to develop reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensors. In present work, CHI was used as immobilization matrix due to its good biocompatibility, high permeability for water and film-forming ability. CNDs were added into the CHI matrix to improve the conductivity of the composite film. Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of the prepared CNDs and SEM images of CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE. As observed in Fig. 2a, the CNDs are nearly spherical and their diameters are about 50nm, which is consistent with the characteristics of the CNDs obtained from the candle soot by other researchers [29,30]. As shown in Fig. 2b, numerous E. coli cells are loaded on the surface of GCE and some of them are stacked together, resulting in a rough electrode surface. Fig. 2c revealing more details of the CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE. It can be seen that the modified GCE exhibits a three-dimensional network structure with compact and porous surface: the rod-shaped E. coli are embedded into the CHI matrix; some polymer-like coverings can be observed on the E. coli surface; the rod-shaped E. coli are embedded into the CHI film; some CNDs are spreading on the surface of E. coli surface. It can be expected that this morphological characteristics may result in high loading of E.coli@TH, and also improve the electron transfer between the E. coli and the electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of different electrochemical biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: To further demenstrate the wraping of TH on the E. coli surface and investigate the viability of E. coli in the immobilized matrix, cyclic voltammetry (CV) behaviours of CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE (line a1 and a2), CHI-CNDs-E.coli-TH/GCE (line b1 and b2) and CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE (line c1 and c2) were conducted in PBS (pH=7, 0.1M) in absence (Fig. 3 a) and presence (Fig. 3b) of respiration substrate (RS). As shown in Fig. 3a, when no RS is used, no redox wave is observed in the CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE (line a1) as expected due to native E. coli cannot exchange electrons with electrode. By contrast, a pair of redox peaks are observed with a redox potential at about −0.1 to −0.2V (vs Ag/AgCl) for the CHI-CNDs-E.coli-TH/GCE (line b1) and CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE (line c1), which can be ascribed to the redox process of mediator TH, revealing that the TH retains its redox properties no matter of simply mixing it with the CHI matrix or wrapping it on the E. coli surface through electrostatic interaction. Fig. 3b shows the CV behaviours when these electrodes were exposed to RS solution. It is noteworthy that the RS solution containing glucose, sodium lactate and sodium succinate, both of which is important nutrient for microbes. And the addition of respiration substrate can improve the metabolism of E. coli and speeds up the electron transfer in the electron transport chain (ETC). As shown in Fig. 3b, almost no change of redox peak current is observed in the CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE (line a2) and CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE (line b2), while an obvious increase of anodic peak currents for the wrapping-type CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE (line c2) appeared, suggesting that wrapping TH on E. coli surface make TH obtain more opportunity to accept electrons from the ETC than simple mixing TH with E. coli and CHI-CNDs matrix since electrostatic interactions make E. coli and TH closely together. For the CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE, the presence of RS speeds up the electron transfer in ETC, resulting in much TH that wrapped on the E. coli surface be reduced, and finally lead to the higher anodic peak current due to the oxidation of the reduced form of TH from the mediated reaction [23]. It is well known electron transfer process is dependent on the distance between the electroactive centers and electrode. Due to the blockage of non-conductive cell wall and plasma membrane, most microbes are unable to transfer electrons to electrode directly [13]. Adjusting the comparative position between redox center and electrode and making their distance as close as possible is an important factor to realized fast electron transfer. Obviously, wrapping TH on the surface of E. coli surface make E. coli and TH closely together, and shorten the electron transfer distance between electroactive centers in ETC and electrode, which makes the electron transfer from E. coli to electrode more easily. Meanwhile, from the above data it can be confirmed that E. coli still keep their viability in spite of wrapped by TH and further immobilized into the CHI-CNDs matrix. All results reveal that direct ‘wrapping’ TH on the surface of E. coli surface through the simple electrostatic interactions is an effective way to co-immobilize mediator and microbe on the electrode.

The CVs of the CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE at different scan rates were shown in Fig. 3c. Inset shows the plots of peak current vs. the scan rate. With the increase of scan rate, the anodic peak potentials move to the more positive direction and the cathodic peak potentials shift to the more negative direction, indicating a quasi-reversible electrochemical process. Besides, a linear relationship of peak current vs. scan rate is revealed, suggesting the electrode reaction is mainly controlled by surface processes.

Furthermore, EIS has been used to characterize the interface properties of the different types modified electrodes. Fig. 3d shows the typical results of AC impedance spectra of bare GCE, no CNDs modified CHI-E.coli@TH/GCE, as well as CNDs modified CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE in 5mMK4[Fe(CN)6]/K3[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1M KCl, respectively. Well defined semi-circles at higher frequencies are obtained at bare GCE and no CNDs imbedded CHI-E. coli@TH/GCE. After the modification by CHI and E. coli@TH on the surface of GCE, the resistance to charge transfer (Rct) decreased from 175Ω from to 130Ω. Given that CHI contains many free protonated amine groups, the Rct decrease may result from the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged amine groups and the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4−, which leads to the pre-concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− on the surface of the electrode. And this Rct decrease phenomenon is also observed in other CHI modified electrodes [33–35]. Moreover, for the CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE, only a linear straight line is observed in the AC impedance spectrum, indicating the warburg impedance is greatly depressed and the electron shuttle between the electrolyte and electrode is intensely accelerated. Based on the mentioned results, it can be concluded that imbedding CNDs into the CHI matrix greatly facilitate the electron transfer in the modified electrode. All these preliminary experiments suggest the present electrochemical system, i.e., the reagentless CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE, would be a promising electrochemical platform for wastewater toxicity assessment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical Performance of the Biosensor for acute total toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the electrochemical responses of the prepared CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE, CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE and CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE to several heavy metal ions. Cd2+, a common toxic heavy metal ion, was chosen to test the analytical performance of these three modified electrodes. As shown in Fig. 4, only week background current can be observed for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE, suggesting there is no electron transfer between the E. coli cells and electrode. By contrast, a 1.4μA steady current is observed for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE, indicating thionine that ‘wrapped’ on the surface of E. coli acts as the “electron wire” to shuttle electrons from the E. coli cells to the electrode. The electron transfer from a low redox potential donor to an acceptor with a higher redox potential is vital in the microbial respiration process for ATP synthesis. NADH is one of the primary electron donors in the electron transport chain [36,37]. The redox potentials for NAD/NADH is −0.32V (vs. NHE), which is lower than 0.06V (vs. NHE), the redox potential of thionine at pH=7. As a result, thionine may firstly be reduced due to its acceptance of electrons from ETC and then oxidized by the electrode to generate the steady current. In comparison, although there is thionine exist, the steady current for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE is about 180μA, which is much lower than the steady current for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE. Based on these mentioned results, it is obvious that wrapping TH on the E. coli cell surface is more efficient for electron wiring than simple adding TH into the immobilization matrix, since electrostatic interactions make E .coli and TH closely together and shorten the electron transfer distance between electroactive centers in ETC and electrode, which makes the electron transfer from E. coli to electrode more easily. Upon adding Cd2+ (15mg/L) to the solution, the steady current decrease promptly for the chitosan-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE, suggesting the respiration of E. coli is inhibited, while no obvious change of the steady current is observed for the CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE and CHI-CNDs-E. coli-TH/GCE. Therefore, wrapping TH on the E. coli surface prompts the electron transfer between E. coli and electrode, which makes it promising to utilize the reagentless CHI-CNDs-E. coli/GCE biosensor for toxicity assessment of heavy metal ions.

Further experiments were carried out to investigate the effect of pH on the steady current of chronoamperometry by using RS solution with different pH levels. As shown in Fig. S3a, the largest steady current is observed at pH=7.0 for different pH levels, indicating that metabolism of E. coli may become more active at neutral condition. Besides, it is noteworthy that the steady current decrease more dramatically in acidic condition than alkaline condition. Given that the pK a of CHI is 6.0, this phenomenon may result from the dissolution of the CHI film in acidic condition [38]. Studies to investigate the dependence of the biosensor response on the CND concentration also performed. The CNDs imbedded in the CHI matrix is used to improve the conductivity of the modified electrode. As shown in Fig. S3b, with the increase of volume of CNDs solution from 10μL to 20μL, the steady current increases from 1.27μA to 1.4μA; however, the further increase of CNDs volume from 20μL to 50μL, the steady current decreases from 1.4μA to 0.93μA. As a result, the CNDs volume should be in a proper range to achieve the best electrochemical response. Thus, in order to achieve the optimized electrochemical performance, pH=7 and 20μL CNDs solution were chosen in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for toxicity assessment of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their high aqueous solubility, heavy metal ions can leach into the environment easily, and then threaten humans’ health through their direct toxicity or bioaccumulation [39]. It has been known that some heavy metal ions are involved in several diseases, such as cancer, chronic inflammation, diabetes, neurological disorders (Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease), etc [40]. Moreover, according to the estimation from the World Health Organization, almost 693 million people had suffered from drinking unsafe water with heavy metal ions as a main kind of pollutants by the end of 2011 [41]. Thus, assessing the ttoxicity of heavy metal ions is meaningful and necessary. The toxicity of four heavy metal ions (Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+) were assessed by the integrated CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE biosensor and the relationships between the inhibitory percentages and the concentration of heavy metal ions are shown in Fig. 5 . According to the inhibitory curves, the calculated IC50 values for Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ are 36.2mg/L, 20.2mgL, 34.3mg/L and 53.2mg/L, respectively, indicating the toxicity order of these four heavy metal ions is Cu2+ >Pb2+ >Cd2+ >Zn2+. Furthermore, we compared the IC50 values achieved by the integrated chitosan-CNDs-E. coli@TH/GCE biosensor with those obtained by other methods, and the results are shown in Table 1 . The IC50 values achieved by our approach are comparable and even better than those obtained by other electrochemical methods based on respiration inhibition of microbes. Nitrification inhibition and bioluminescence inhibition are two methods which have been standardized by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) for toxicity assessment. The comparison shows that the IC50 values achieved by our method are also comparable with those of these two standard methods. Besides, it should be noted that there are some differences in the toxicity order of these four heavy metal ions obtained by different methods, which might from the different experiment procedures of each method and the diverse characteristics of various microbes [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the biosensor for toxicity assessment of three wastewater samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practicability of the reagentless CHI-CNDs-E.coli@TH/GCE biosensor, we have further evaluated the toxicity of three wastewater samples, i.e., effluent from landfill, wastewater from chemical laboratory and electroplating wastewater. As shown in Fig. S4, the inhibitions by effluent from landfill, wastewater from chemical laboratory and electroplating wastewater are 33.3%, 37.5% and 55.1%, respectively, indicating electroplating wastewater is most toxic among these wastewater samples. The successful evaluation of toxicity of these three wastewater samples suggests it is promising to utilize this reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor for practical application. However, given the potential growth and prolification of E. coli during the toxicity assessment, this electrochemical microbial biosensor is preferred for short-term toxicity monitoring.

An effective way to prepare the reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor has been developed by direct wrapping TH on the surface of E. coli through the simple electrostatic interactions, making E. coli and TH closely together, and therefore shortening the electron transfer distance between electroactive centers in ETC and electrode. CNDs with excellent electrical properties are embedded into the CHI matrix to improve the conductivity of the modified electrode, and further enhance the electron transfer from the microbial to the electrode. CNDs imbedded into the CHI matrix improve the conductivity of the integrated electrode. Comparing to simple mixing thionine with the immobilization matrix, wrapping TH on the E. coli surface is more efficient to wire the E. coli to the electrode. To our best knowledge, this method has never been reported. The toxicity of Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ are successful evaluated and the corresponding IC50 values for these four heavy metal ions are 36.2mg/L, 20.2mg/L, 34.3mg/L and 53.2mg/L, respectively. The toxicity of three wastewater samples is evaluated successfully by the developed electrochemical microbial biosensor. In summary, the proposed method offering a simple and convenient methodology for co-immobilization of mediator and microbe, and also provides a new alternative to constructing integrated and reagentless electrochemical microbial biosensor for wastewater toxicity assessment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the supports of the Key Project of Beijing Natural Science Foundation (No. 2120002), the International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China (Most-Japan joint project, No. 2013DFG50150), and the Natural Foundation of Sciences of the People's Republic of China (No. 21175144). We quite appreciate the help of Professor Ying Liu from College of Life Sciences, Northwest A&F University on knowledge of exoelectrogens.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.10.174.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article
